INDIAN SCANDAL LIKELY TO


COST HITCHCOCK HIS HEAD

pages.raw_text:
holder in the same trust company as
Chairman Bixby, namely, the Canadian
Valley. At the time the company
was organized it was part of the agent S
duties to pass on the value of the
lands which the Indians were to sell
and which it was proposed the trust
company was to buy.


The Indian territory scandal is like
ly to cost Secretary Hitchcock his
place in the cabinet. Whatever abuses
have grown up have developed dur


ing Mr.. Hitchcock administration,
and in consequence of lax supervls-
ion. The Indian inspector, who is
the secretary's personal representative
on the ground, is as deep in the mire
as the agent or any of the Dawes
commissioners. Instead of reporting
facts to the secretary, he permitted
members of his Office force to specu
late in town site lots which it was his
duty to plat and appraise. Either the
secretary of the interior has been Woe
fully ignorant of goings on in the Indian
territory, or he has been culpably neg
ligent.


But what win reflect more damag-
ingly on Secretary Hitchcock is the
fact that the inquiry had to come from
the president. The interior depart
ment never has taken any step tc
purge itself of the accusations which
have been brought. As soon as tbs
charge of crookedness was brought
against postal officials an official Of
the postoffice department Vas designat-
ed to probe it to the bottom. So, as
soon as Brosius charged that the
agents Of the department of justice
had been grating, three special inspec-
tors were hurried to the scene and a
thorough investigation made, which
resulted in a number of discharges and
arrests, But all this time the secretary
OF interior continued 10 ignore charges
ana rumors, and all his officials and
